title: Dog Loss of Balance on Stairs: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog is losing balance on stairs, the urgency level, and how videos help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Loss of balance (ataxia) in dogs is a medium-priority concern that requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 hours to rule out serious neurological or inner ear conditions. Capturing a video of your dog's gait can significantly assist your vet in making an accurate diagnosis.

What is loss of balance or ataxia in dogs?

Loss of balance, medically known as ataxia, occurs when there is a lack of coordination in the limbs, head, or trunk. When a dog struggles on stairs, it often appears as stumbling, swaying, or a sudden reluctance to climb. This condition can stem from various sources, including inner ear issues (vestibular system), spinal cord compression, or neurological dysfunction.

Is it an emergency if my dog is losing their balance?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While a sudden loss of coordination is concerning, it is generally considered a medium-priority situation unless accompanied by extreme distress, seizures, or collapse.
  • Veterinary Consultation: Because stairs pose a significant fall risk, you should consult a veterinarian within 24 hours to rule out serious conditions like 'Old Dog' Vestibular Disease or intervertebral disc disease (IVDD).

How can a photo or video of my dog's movement help with triage?

  • Capture Intermittent Symptoms: Video records gait issues that may not be occurring by the time you reach the clinic.
  • Identify Clinical Signs: It allows the vet to see specific movements, such as a head tilt or abnormal eye flickering.
  • Improve Diagnostic Accuracy: Visual evidence helps the veterinary team differentiate between musculoskeletal pain and complex neurological issues.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Loss of balance on stairs can be attributed to several neurologic conditions. Cerebellar disorders can result in incoordination (dysmetria) of the head, trunk, and limbs, often presenting with ataxia. Brain stem disorders may cause cranial nerve dysfunction, postural disturbances, or vestibular dysfunction; severely affected animals may exhibit impaired consciousness, weakness, and proprioceptive gait disturbances. Spinal cord disorders may produce weakness, motor dysfunction, or proprioceptive deficits of the limbs. Vestibular dysfunction may cause ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling. Evaluation should include assessment of posture, gait, and postural reactions such as conscious proprioceptive positioning to detect subtle neurologic deficits.

Chapter: Neurology, Neuromuscular

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1222)

faqs.question: Could my dog have had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While strokes can occur in dogs, loss of balance is more frequently caused by Vestibular Disease, which mimics stroke-like symptoms but has a different treatment path.

faqs.question: What should I do to keep my dog safe right now?
faqs.answer: Block off all stairways with baby gates and avoid letting your dog jump on or off furniture. Use a harness or a towel under their belly as a sling to assist them when they need to go outside.

faqs.question: Can arthritis cause balance issues on stairs?
faqs.answer: Yes, severe joint pain or weakness from arthritis can make a dog appear unbalanced or hesitant, though this is usually a more gradual onset than neurological ataxia.
